revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese new religious movement based in Noda, Chiba}} {{Infobox organization | name = Reiha-no-Hikari | native_name = 霊波之光 | native_name_lang = ja | abbreviation = RHK | formation = 1954 | founder = Hase Yoshio | type = [[Religious corporation (Japan)|Religious corporation]] | purpose = Salvation of humankind; world peace | headquarters = Yamasaki 2683-1, [[Noda, Chiba|Noda]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan | region_served = Japan | website = {{URL|https://www.rhk.jp/}} }}
'''Reiha-no-Hikari''' (Japanese: 霊波之光, lit. ‘‘Light of the Spiritual Wave’’) is a Japanese [[new religious movement]] headquartered in [[Noda, Chiba]].<ref name="Kokugakuin">{{cite web |title=Reiha no Hikari Kyōkai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9800 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref> The group teaches that relief from illness and suffering and miraculous benefits can be obtained through what it calls “spiritual waves” (''reiha''). It identifies its goals as “salvation of humankind” and “the realization of world peace.”<ref name="RHKguide">{{cite web |title=参拝ガイド（Visitor guide） |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
The founder (first leader) was '''Hase Yoshio''' (1915–1984; secular name 長谷義雄). Leadership later passed within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Overview == Reiha-no-Hikari teaches that through a form of divine power it terms “spiritual waves,” adherents can be saved from illness and other troubles and experience miracles. The movement refers to its founder as a manifestation of the divine and uses the honorific title ''Goshugojin-sama'' (“Venerable Guardian Deity”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
The group uses its own calendar called the ‘‘Reiha calendar,’’ which counts from the founder’s birth year (1915). Its scripture is called the ''Gosho'' (“Honorable Writing”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Characteristics == Prospective members “tie a bond” (''otsunagari'') with the organization at the headquarters or local branches. The organization describes member dues and offerings using terms such as “gratitude offerings,” and provides a household ''ofuda'' (paper talisman) upon enrollment.<ref name="RHKguide" />
Members engage in prayer, volunteer activities, and proselytizing, and may testify to “experiences” (''taiken'') that they interpret as miracles. The organization publishes internal talks and newsletters to members via a password-protected website.<ref name="RHKguide" />
== Headquarters and facilities == The headquarters complex is in Yamasaki, Noda, Chiba. A notable building historically associated with the organization has been known as the ‘‘Tenshikaku’’ (“Castle of Salvation”).<ref name="tokyofox">{{cite web |last=Tokyo Fox |title=Is That Another Castle In Chiba? All Is Not Quite What It Seems |url=https://tokyofox.net/2020/05/30/is-that-another-castle-in-chiba-all-is-not-quite-what-it-seems/ |website=Tokyo Fox |date=May 30, 2020 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Branch network in Japan == * Headquarters: Noda, Chiba * Regional branches include Sapporo, Sendai, Koriyama, Yokohama, Shizuoka, Kansai (Akashi), Kōchi, Kitakyūshū, and Okinawa; selected branches administer local sub-branches (e.g., Kushiro, Asahikawa, Niigata, Nagoya, Matsuyama).{{cn|date=November 2025}}<ref name="RHKguide" />
== History == * March 7, 1954: Founding based on revelations said to have been received by Hase Yoshio.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * February 1956: Establishment of the “Reiha-no-Hikari Devotional Association.”{{cn|date=November 2025}} * September 23, 1957: Recognition as a [[Religious corporation (Japan)|religious corporation]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} * December 31, 1957: A temporary main center was established in Mabashi, Matsudo, Chiba.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1969: Relocation to the present site in Noda; completion of an early sanctuary and hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 2, 1975: Completion of the Tenshikaku building.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1982–1983: Publication of the scripture ''Gosho'' and the origin narrative series ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”).{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 19, 1984: Death of founder Hase Yoshio; succession of leadership within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * June 8, 1996: The movement built the pedestrian “Fureai Bridge” over the Tone Canal and donated it to Noda City (management transferred to Nagareyama City in 2013).<ref>{{cite news |title=［しおかぜ］利根運河にふれあい橋――野田市／千葉 |work=毎日新聞 (''Mainichi Shimbun'') |date=August 7, 1996 |language=ja |location=Chiba (local edition)}}</ref> * December 7, 2003: Completion of a new sanctuary; July 2, 2015: completion of a new worship hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * June 26, 2022: Completion of the Shizuoka Branch facility.<ref>{{cite web |title=静岡支部 落成 |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Events == The organization observes three annual grand festivals: '''March 7''' (''Goseishin-sai''), '''June 8''' (''Goseiryō-sai''), and '''July 2''' (''Goseitan-sai'').{{cn|date=November 2025}} On June 8, memorial services for ancestors and ''mizuko'' (deceased infants) are offered free of charge to visitors, including non-members.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== Media == Founder Hase Yoshio directed a film series titled ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”) dramatizing his period of spiritual training. The organization later produced testimonial “experience” films under leadership supervision.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== See also == * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Federation of New Religious Organizations of Japan]]
== Notes == {{notelist}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.rhk.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * [[Commons:Category:Reiha-no-Hikari|Media related to Reiha-no-Hikari]] on Wikimedia Commons
[[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1954]] [[Category:Organizations based in Chiba Prefecture]]

The movement refers to its founder as a manifestation of the divine and uses the honorific title ''Goshugojin-sama'' (“Venerable Guardian Deity”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
The group uses its own calendar called the ‘‘Reiha calendar,’’ which counts from the founder’s birth year (1915). Its scripture is called the ''Gosho'' (“Honorable Writing”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Characteristics == Prospective members “tie a bond” (''otsunagari'') with the organization at the headquarters or local branches. The organization describes member dues and offerings using terms such as “gratitude offerings,” and provides a household ''ofuda'' (paper talisman) upon enrollment.<ref name="RHKguide" />
Members engage in prayer, volunteer activities, and proselytizing, and may testify to “experiences” (''taiken'') that they interpret as miracles. The organization publishes internal talks and newsletters to members via a password-protected website.<ref name="RHKguide" />
== Headquarters and facilities == The headquarters complex is in Yamasaki, Noda, Chiba. A notable building historically associated with the organization has been known as the ‘‘Tenshikaku’’ (“Castle of Salvation”).<ref name="tokyofox">{{cite web |last=Tokyo Fox |title=Is That Another Castle In Chiba? All Is Not Quite What It Seems |url=https://tokyofox.net/2020/05/30/is-that-another-castle-in-chiba-all-is-not-quite-what-it-seems/ |website=Tokyo Fox |date=May 30, 2020 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Branch network in Japan == * Headquarters: Noda, Chiba * Regional branches include Sapporo, Sendai, Koriyama, Yokohama, Shizuoka, Kansai (Akashi), Kōchi, Kitakyūshū, and Okinawa; selected branches administer local sub-branches (e.g., Kushiro, Asahikawa, Niigata, Nagoya, Matsuyama).{{cn|date=November 2025}}<ref name="RHKguide" />
== History == * March 7, 1954: Founding based on revelations said to have been received by Hase Yoshio.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * February 1956: Establishment of the “Reiha-no-Hikari Devotional Association.”{{cn|date=November 2025}} * September 23, 1957: Recognition as a [[Religious corporation (Japan)|religious corporation]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} * December 31, 1957: A temporary main center was established in Mabashi, Matsudo, Chiba.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1969: Relocation to the present site in Noda; completion of an early sanctuary and hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 2, 1975: Completion of the Tenshikaku building.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1982–1983: Publication of the scripture ''Gosho'' and the origin narrative series ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”).{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 19, 1984: Death of founder Hase Yoshio; succession of leadership within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * June 8, 1996: The movement built the pedestrian “Fureai Bridge” over the Tone Canal and donated it to Noda City (management transferred to Nagareyama City in 2013).<ref>{{cite news |title=［しおかぜ］利根運河にふれあい橋――野田市／千葉 |work=毎日新聞 (''Mainichi Shimbun'') |date=August 7, 1996 |language=ja |location=Chiba (local edition)}}</ref> * December 7, 2003: Completion of a new sanctuary; July 2, 2015: completion of a new worship hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * June 26, 2022: Completion of the Shizuoka Branch facility.<ref>{{cite web |title=静岡支部 落成 |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Events == The organization observes three annual grand festivals: '''March 7''' (''Goseishin-sai''), '''June 8''' (''Goseiryō-sai''), and '''July 2''' (''Goseitan-sai'').{{cn|date=November 2025}} On June 8, memorial services for ancestors and ''mizuko'' (deceased infants) are offered free of charge to visitors, including non-members.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== Media == Founder Hase Yoshio directed a film series titled ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”) dramatizing his period of spiritual training. The organization later produced testimonial “experience” films under leadership supervision.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== See also == * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Federation of New Religious Organizations of Japan]]
== Notes == {{notelist}}
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.rhk.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * [[Commons:Category:Reiha-no-Hikari|Media related to Reiha-no-Hikari]] on Wikimedia Commons * [https://sites.google.com/site/rhkreihanohikarikyokai/testimonials English site] * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9800 Encyclopedia of Shinto entry]

All Is Not Quite What It Seems |url=https://tokyofox.net/2020/05/30/is-that-another-castle-in-chiba-all-is-not-quite-what-it-seems/ |website=Tokyo Fox |date=May 30, 2020 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Branch network in Japan == * Headquarters: Noda, Chiba * Regional branches include Sapporo, Sendai, Koriyama, Yokohama, Shizuoka, Kansai (Akashi), Kōchi, Kitakyūshū, and Okinawa; selected branches administer local sub-branches (e.g., Kushiro, Asahikawa, Niigata, Nagoya, Matsuyama).{{cn|date=November 2025}}<ref name="RHKguide" />
== History == * March 7, 1954: Founding based on revelations said to have been received by Hase Yoshio.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * February 1956: Establishment of the “Reiha-no-Hikari Devotional Association.”{{cn|date=November 2025}} * September 23, 1957: Recognition as a [[Religious corporation (Japan)|religious corporation]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} * December 31, 1957: A temporary main center was established in Mabashi, Matsudo, Chiba.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1969: Relocation to the present site in Noda; completion of an early sanctuary and hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 2, 1975: Completion of the Tenshikaku building.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1982–1983: Publication of the scripture ''Gosho'' and the origin narrative series ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”).{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 19, 1984: Death of founder Hase Yoshio; succession of leadership within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * June 8, 1996: The movement built the pedestrian “Fureai Bridge” over the Tone Canal and donated it to Noda City (management transferred to Nagareyama City in 2013).<ref>{{cite news |title=［しおかぜ］利根運河にふれあい橋――野田市／千葉 |work=毎日新聞 (''Mainichi Shimbun'') |date=August 7, 1996 |language=ja |location=Chiba (local edition)}}</ref> * December 7, 2003: Completion of a new sanctuary; July 2, 2015: completion of a new worship hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * June 26, 2022: Completion of the Shizuoka Branch facility.<ref>{{cite web |title=静岡支部 落成 |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Events == The organization observes three annual grand festivals: '''March 7''' (''Goseishin-sai''), '''June 8''' (''Goseiryō-sai''), and '''July 2''' (''Goseitan-sai'').{{cn|date=November 2025}} On June 8, memorial services for ancestors and ''mizuko'' (deceased infants) are offered free of charge to visitors, including non-members.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== Media == Founder Hase Yoshio directed a film series titled ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”) dramatizing his period of spiritual training. The organization later produced testimonial “experience” films under leadership supervision.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== See also == * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Federation of New Religious Organizations of Japan]]

== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.rhk.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * [[Commons:Category:Reiha-no-Hikari|Media related to Reiha-no-Hikari]] on Wikimedia Commons * [https://sites.google.com/site/rhkreihanohikarikyokai/testimonials English site] * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9800 Encyclopedia of Shinto entry]
[[Category:Japanese new religions]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1954]] [[Category:Organizations based in Chiba Prefecture]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Short description|Japanese new religious movement based in Noda, Chiba}} {{Infobox organization | name = Reiha-no-Hikari | native_name = 霊波之光 | native_name_lang = ja | abbreviation = RHK | formation = 1954 | founder = Hase Yoshio | type = [[Religious corporation (Japan)|Religious corporation]] | purpose = Salvation of humankind; world peace | headquarters = Yamasaki 2683-1, [[Noda, Chiba|Noda]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan | region_served = Japan | website = {{URL|https://www.rhk.jp/}} }}
'''Reiha-no-Hikari''' (Japanese: 霊波之光, lit. ‘‘Light of the Spiritual Wave’’) is a Japanese [[new religious movement]] headquartered in [[Noda, Chiba]].<ref name="Kokugakuin">{{cite web |title=Reiha no Hikari Kyōkai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9800 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref> The group teaches that relief from illness and suffering and miraculous benefits can be obtained through what it calls “spiritual waves” (''reiha''). It identifies its goals as “salvation of humankind” and “the realization of world peace.”<ref name="RHKguide">{{cite web |title=参拝ガイド（Visitor guide） |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
The founder (first leader) was '''Hase Yoshio''' (1915–1984; secular name 長谷義雄). Leadership later passed within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Overview == Reiha-no-Hikari teaches that through a form of divine power it terms “spiritual waves,” adherents can be saved from illness and other troubles and experience miracles. The movement refers to its founder as a manifestation of the divine and uses the honorific title ''Goshugojin-sama'' (“Venerable Guardian Deity”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
The group uses its own calendar called the ‘‘Reiha calendar,’’ which counts from the founder’s birth year (1915). Its scripture is called the ''Gosho'' (“Honorable Writing”).<ref name="Kokugakuin" />
== Characteristics == Prospective members “tie a bond” (''otsunagari'') with the organization at the headquarters or local branches. The organization describes member dues and offerings using terms such as “gratitude offerings,” and provides a household ''ofuda'' (paper talisman) upon enrollment.<ref name="RHKguide" />
Members engage in prayer, volunteer activities, and proselytizing, and may testify to “experiences” (''taiken'') that they interpret as miracles. The organization publishes internal talks and newsletters to members via a password-protected website.<ref name="RHKguide" />
== Headquarters and facilities == The headquarters complex is in Yamasaki, Noda, Chiba. A notable building historically associated with the organization has been known as the ‘‘Tenshikaku’’ (“Castle of Salvation”).<ref name="tokyofox">{{cite web |last=Tokyo Fox |title=Is That Another Castle In Chiba? All Is Not Quite What It Seems |url=https://tokyofox.net/2020/05/30/is-that-another-castle-in-chiba-all-is-not-quite-what-it-seems/ |website=Tokyo Fox |date=May 30, 2020 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Branch network in Japan == * Headquarters: Noda, Chiba * Regional branches include Sapporo, Sendai, Koriyama, Yokohama, Shizuoka, Kansai (Akashi), Kōchi, Kitakyūshū, and Okinawa; selected branches administer local sub-branches (e.g., Kushiro, Asahikawa, Niigata, Nagoya, Matsuyama).{{cn|date=November 2025}}<ref name="RHKguide" />
== History == * March 7, 1954: Founding based on revelations said to have been received by Hase Yoshio.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * February 1956: Establishment of the “Reiha-no-Hikari Devotional Association.”{{cn|date=November 2025}} * September 23, 1957: Recognition as a [[Religious corporation (Japan)|religious corporation]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} * December 31, 1957: A temporary main center was established in Mabashi, Matsudo, Chiba.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1969: Relocation to the present site in Noda; completion of an early sanctuary and hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 2, 1975: Completion of the Tenshikaku building.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1982–1983: Publication of the scripture ''Gosho'' and the origin narrative series ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”).{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 19, 1984: Death of founder Hase Yoshio; succession of leadership within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * June 8, 1996: The movement built the pedestrian “Fureai Bridge” over the Tone Canal and donated it to Noda City (management transferred to Nagareyama City in 2013).<ref>{{cite news |title=［しおかぜ］利根運河にふれあい橋――野田市／千葉 |work=毎日新聞 (''Mainichi Shimbun'') |date=August 7, 1996 |language=ja |location=Chiba (local edition)}}</ref> * December 7, 2003: Completion of a new sanctuary; July 2, 2015: completion of a new worship hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * June 26, 2022: Completion of the Shizuoka Branch facility.<ref>{{cite web |title=静岡支部 落成 |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Events == The organization observes three annual grand festivals: '''March 7''' (''Goseishin-sai''), '''June 8''' (''Goseiryō-sai''), and '''July 2''' (''Goseitan-sai'').{{cn|date=November 2025}} On June 8, memorial services for ancestors and ''mizuko'' (deceased infants) are offered free of charge to visitors, including non-members.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== Media == Founder Hase Yoshio directed a film series titled ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”) dramatizing his period of spiritual training. The organization later produced testimonial “experience” films under leadership supervision.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== See also == * [[Japanese new religions]] * [[Federation of New Religious Organizations of Japan]]

== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://www.rhk.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * [[Commons:Category:Reiha-no-Hikari|Media related to Reiha-no-Hikari]] on Wikimedia Commons * [https://sites.google.com/site/rhkreihanohikarikyokai/testimonials English site] * [https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9800 Encyclopedia of Shinto entry]

All Is Not Quite What It Seems |url=https://tokyofox.net/2020/05/30/is-that-another-castle-in-chiba-all-is-not-quite-what-it-seems/ |website=Tokyo Fox |date=May 30, 2020 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Branch network in Japan == * Headquarters: Noda, Chiba * Regional branches include Sapporo, Sendai, Koriyama, Yokohama, Shizuoka, Kansai (Akashi), Kōchi, Kitakyūshū, and Okinawa; selected branches administer local sub-branches (e.g., Kushiro, Asahikawa, Niigata, Nagoya, Matsuyama).{{cn|date=November 2025}}<ref name="RHKguide" />
== History == * March 7, 1954: Founding based on revelations said to have been received by Hase Yoshio.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * February 1956: Establishment of the “Reiha-no-Hikari Devotional Association.”{{cn|date=November 2025}} * September 23, 1957: Recognition as a [[Religious corporation (Japan)|religious corporation]].{{cn|date=November 2025}} * December 31, 1957: A temporary main center was established in Mabashi, Matsudo, Chiba.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1969: Relocation to the present site in Noda; completion of an early sanctuary and hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 2, 1975: Completion of the Tenshikaku building.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * 1982–1983: Publication of the scripture ''Gosho'' and the origin narrative series ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”).{{cn|date=November 2025}} * March 19, 1984: Death of founder Hase Yoshio; succession of leadership within the Hase family.<ref name="Kokugakuin" /> * June 8, 1996: The movement built the pedestrian “Fureai Bridge” over the Tone Canal and donated it to Noda City (management transferred to Nagareyama City in 2013).<ref>{{cite news |title=［しおかぜ］利根運河にふれあい橋――野田市／千葉 |work=毎日新聞 (''Mainichi Shimbun'') |date=August 7, 1996 |language=ja |location=Chiba (local edition)}}</ref> * December 7, 2003: Completion of a new sanctuary; July 2, 2015: completion of a new worship hall.{{cn|date=November 2025}} * June 26, 2022: Completion of the Shizuoka Branch facility.<ref>{{cite web |title=静岡支部 落成 |url=https://www.rhk.jp/ |website=Reiha-no-Hikari official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Events == The organization observes three annual grand festivals: '''March 7''' (''Goseishin-sai''), '''June 8''' (''Goseiryō-sai''), and '''July 2''' (''Goseitan-sai'').{{cn|date=November 2025}} On June 8, memorial services for ancestors and ''mizuko'' (deceased infants) are offered free of charge to visitors, including non-members.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
== Media == Founder Hase Yoshio directed a film series titled ''Kami e no michi'' (“The Way to God”) dramatizing his period of spiritual training. The organization later produced testimonial “experience” films under leadership supervision.{{cn|date=November 2025}}
